text: An erroneous system state that can lead to system behavior that is unexpected is called:
explanation: A system error is an erroneous state that can lead to unexpected system behavior.

text: Fault avoidance is the application of verification and validation techniques used to discover and remove faults in a system before it is deployed.
explanation: True. Fault avoidance focuses on preventing faults before deployment using V&V techniques.

text: Reliability measures are system attributes that define the number of failures that are acceptable during normal use of the system.
explanation: False. Reliability measures define probability and rate of failures, not acceptable number of failures.

text: ______ probability that the system will fail when a service request is made.
explanation: POFOD (Probability of Failure on Demand) measures the probability that the system will fail when a request is made.

text: Among the benefits of creating a reliability specification is:
options: It provides a basis for assessing when to stop testing a system
It reduces the cost of the system
It can be created after the system is implemented
explanation: Reliability specifications help determine when sufficient testing has been achieved.

text: A specialized system associated with some other control system that can take emergency action if a failure occurs is called:
explanation: A protection system monitors and takes emergency action if failure occurs.

text: The fault-tolerant solutions studied in class have as a core concept:
explanation: Redundancy is the core concept behind fault-tolerant systems.

text: Which is true of a self-monitoring system?
options: It should monitor its own operations and take action if inconsistencies are detected.
Extensive use should be made of diversity so hardware failure will not lead to identical results.
explanation: Self-monitoring systems use monitoring, diversity, and multiple channels.

text: An example of a validity check is range checking to determine if an input is acceptable.
explanation: True. Range checking is a classic example of input validation.

text: A reasonable exception handling strategy is to:
options: Push the problem to the calling unit
Try to fix the problem in the handler
Pass the problem to a run-time support system
explanation: All listed strategies may be reasonable depending on context.

text: In C, assuming int nums[100] is valid, the statement nums[105]; may result in addressing memory outside the array bounds.
explanation: True. C does not enforce bounds checking at runtime.

text: To recover from most post-delivery software faults it is usually enough to:
options: Throw exceptions and generate detailed error messages
Simply restart the program at a known stable point
Put the system in a safe state if an anomaly is detected
explanation: Restarting from a known stable checkpoint is often sufficient.

text: The idea behind checkpoint and restart systems is similar to the Memento design pattern.
explanation: True. Both involve saving state and restoring it later.

text: Programming for reliability includes:
options: Naming all real-world constants in programs
explanation: Checking bounds and naming constants improve reliability.

text: Reliability testing is essentially the same as normal software testing.
explanation: False. Reliability testing focuses on failure rates and operational profiles.

text: An operational profile defines:
options: A set of classes of expected inputs
The expected frequency of input classes
The time spent in operational modules
explanation: Operational profile defines input classes and their expected frequencies.

text: By far the largest source of threats to software security results from vulnerability in:
explanation: Application software vulnerabilities are the largest threat source.

text: Something of value which has to be protected is called:
explanation: An asset is something of value that must be protected.

text: Circumstances that have potential to cause loss or harm are called:
explanation: A threat has the potential to cause harm.

text: A weakness in a computer-based system that may be exploited is called:
explanation: A vulnerability is a weakness that can be exploited.

text: A threat that makes part of the system unavailable is:
explanation: Interruption makes a system or asset unavailable.

text: A threat that allows an attacker to gain access to an asset is:
explanation: Interception allows unauthorized access.

text: Security requirements are classified into risk avoidance, risk detection and risk mitigation.
explanation: True. These are common classifications of security requirements.

text: A misuse case describes how an unauthorized user of a system might attack it.
explanation: True. Misuse cases model potential attacks.

text: A key to providing system security and reliability is creating an appropriate architecture.
explanation: True. Architecture strongly influences security and reliability.
